title: When Monday opens +2%
question: When a market opens a Monday up +2% or more versus the prior close, does it tell you anything about the rest of the week - continuation, reversal, or nothing?
finding: A +2% Monday open is not a follow-through signal, in any market I tested. Single stocks (67,808 Monday gaps across 5,708 names, survivorship-aware incl. delisted): the rest of the week is a coin flip statistically identical to an ordinary Monday - forward %-positive 48.7/50.3/51.1 at +1/+2/+5d vs an ordinary Monday's 48.8/50.1/50.8 - and nothing clears a date-clustered confidence interval. The cleanest framing is de-drifted: a +2% gap-up Monday clusters on broad up-market days, so the stock just inherits the market's move that day and the stock-specific excess is ~0. Indices/ETFs (1,060 gaps; deep history lifts the S&P proxy from 4 qualifying Mondays to 21) show only a small next-day (Tuesday) give-back, and even that is fragile - it lives in the 2008/2020 crises (ex-those-years mean -0.14%, CI includes 0; one-obs-per-date t=-1.4; fails a multiple-comparisons adjustment). 'Monday' itself is not special: a +2% gap behaves the same on any weekday, in direction and - once the horizon is equalized to four sessions - in range (the apparent 'wider Monday week' was a window-length artifact, since a Monday has more days left in the week). Regime/size conditioning is exploratory only (4 of 72 cells clear their CI). The one reliable consequence is a wide, two-sided rest-of-week (~+/-4% of swing both ways; ~90% of gap weeks both rally above and dip below Monday's close) - volatility, not direction. A skeptical re-implementation from scratch reproduced the null and forced three honest downgrades (the index fade, the range claim, the conditioning), all of which strengthened the 'uninformative' verdict.
